preamble: The transformation of the voiced velar plosive /g/ into the voiced alveolo-palatal fricative /ʒ/ is a common historical sound change, primarily driven by palatalization and lenition. This shift typically occurs when /g/ is followed by front vowels or palatal glides, which pull the articulation forward and weaken the stop closure into a fricative.

examples.note: in morphophonological alternations before certain front vowel suffixes, such as the verbal stem extension -i.
before front vowels *e, *ē, *i, *ī during the First Palatalization.
a plausible historical development from Semitic /g/ to /ʒ/ or /dʒ/.

sources: Vocoid-driven processes: Palatalization and glide hardening in Greek and its dialects
Palatalization of Velars: A Major Link of Old English and Old Frisian - Stephen Laker
Velar palatalization in Russian and artificial grammar: Constraints on models of morphophonology - ResearchGate
